name: Polars
what_it_does: Fast multi-threaded DataFrame library written in Rust with Python and Node.js bindings, using Apache Arrow columnar memory format and lazy query evaluation for high-performance data manipulation.
use_cases: Processing large datasets in agent pipelines that exceed Pandas performance limits — Polars is 5-50x faster on many operations
Memory-efficient ETL transforms in agent workflows where columnar Arrow format reduces overhead vs Pandas
Lazy query evaluation (LazyFrame) that optimizes multi-step agent data pipelines before execution
Parallel data processing in agent orchestration without GIL contention — Polars uses true Rust parallelism
Interoperability with Apache Arrow ecosystem (DuckDB, PyArrow, DataFusion) for zero-copy data passing between agent tools
not_for: Teams heavily invested in the Pandas API — Polars API is intentionally different and not a drop-in replacement
Environments where Rust-compiled binaries cause deployment or compliance issues
Simple one-off data exploration where Pandas' wider ecosystem of tutorials and community answers is more valuable
best_when: You need maximum DataFrame performance in Python with parallel execution, and your agent pipeline processes datasets where Pandas is a bottleneck or memory constraint.
avoid_when: You need broad compatibility with the Pandas ecosystem (scikit-learn, matplotlib direct integration, legacy code) — Polars requires explicit conversion and has a different API.

auth.notes: Library — no auth required to install or use. Cloud storage credentials (S3, GCS) are handled by the respective cloud SDK, not Polars.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free and open source — MIT licensed, no usage limits
pricing.notes: Polars is MIT-licensed open source. Polars Cloud (managed service) is a separate commercial offering; the core library has no commercial restrictions.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Polars error messages are generally precise — schema mismatch errors include expected vs actual types, and expression errors point to the specific expression that failed. LazyFrame errors are raised at collect() time, which can make the source of an error less obvious in long pipelines.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Polars operations are immutable by design — all transforms return new DataFrames/LazyFrames. This makes agent pipelines naturally idempotent as long as source data is stable.
known_agent_gotchas: LazyFrame operations do not execute until .collect() is called — agents must call .collect() to materialize results; inspecting a LazyFrame without collecting shows no rows
Polars API is intentionally not Pandas-compatible — agent code written for Pandas cannot be ported without rewriting (no inplace=, different groupby syntax, no .iloc)
Null handling differs from Pandas: Polars uses null (not NaN) for missing values, and NaN is a float value not a missing value indicator — agents doing null checks must use is_null() not isnan()
String expressions use pl.col('x').str.* namespace — agents generating dynamic expressions must use the Polars expression DSL, not Python string methods directly
Polars releases break APIs more frequently than Pandas due to rapid development — pin versions in agent environments and test after upgrades

security_score_components.security_notes: Polars is a local computation library with a small attack surface. No pickle deserialization (unlike Pandas) — serialization uses Arrow IPC or Parquet, both safer formats. Rust implementation reduces memory safety vulnerabilities. Cloud I/O modules delegate credentials to cloud SDKs. No known RCE vectors in the library itself.
